metadata.summary: Modern JS/TS/Python development standards 2026: TypeScript 5 strict, React 19 server components, Next.js 15 caching, Python 3.13, ruff tooling, AI-assisted dev guardrails.

content.title: Software Development Best Practices 2026
content.summary: A navigation hub for modern JS/TS/Python development standards as of 2026: TypeScript 5 strict config, React 19 server components, Next.js 15 patterns, Python 3.13 features, and AI-assisted development guidelines. Always load the relevant sub-doc — reading the hub alone gives only a table of contents.

why.p: Stack currency directly affects build-time performance, type safety, and developer velocity. The 2026 baseline documents which features are now default (TS strict mode, React server-first, ruff over flake8), which are adopted gradually (exactOptionalPropertyTypes), and which AI-tooling practices reduce rather than increase rework. Quarterly review against this hub prevents stack drift.

list.item: Auditing whether a project's stack aligns with current standards (TS version, eslint config, Python tooling).
Generating the standards section of CONTRIBUTING.md or AGENTS.md for a new project.
Choosing which React 19 / Next.js 15 / Python 3.13 features to adopt in a modernization sprint.
Defining an AI-assisted dev playbook: tool selection, prompt patterns, AI test generation guardrails.
Deep language-specific patterns (services, views, handlers) — see dev-methodologies-practices.
Architecture decisions (monolith vs microservice) — see dev-methodologies-architecture.
When the project has a stricter or newer house standard than this 2026 baseline — defer to that.
One-shot bug fixes — load only the specific section, not the full hub.

section.name: typescript-5-strict-configuration-2026-baseline
section.p: Recommended TS 5 strict flags, module resolution settings, and advanced type patterns for 2026.

rule.statement: Enable strict, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, noImplicitOverride, exactOptionalPropertyTypes, and verbatimModuleSyntax in new projects; migrate legacy code under per-file // @ts-strict-check before flipping the global flag.
rule.rationale: Flipping global strict in an existing codebase without budget creates hundreds of errors at once. Per-file gradual adoption lets the team absorb changes incrementally.

section.reference: Full TS 5 strict baseline with all 2026 recommended flags.

example.title: TypeScript 5 strict configuration
example.code: { "compilerOptions": { "target": "ES2023", "module": "ESNext", "moduleResolution": "bundler", "strict": true, "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true, "noImplicitOverride": true, "exactOptionalPropertyTypes": true, "noPropertyAccessFromIndexSignature": true, "verbatimModuleSyntax": true, "isolatedModules": true, "skipLibCheck": true } }

rule.statement: Use moduleResolution: "bundler" for projects targeting Vite, Next.js, or tsup; use "node16" or "nodenext" for pure Node projects without a bundler.
rule.rationale: "bundler" allows extensionless imports that bundlers resolve; "node16" requires .js extensions in import paths for native ESM compliance.

rule.statement: With verbatimModuleSyntax enabled, always use import type for type-only imports; the compiler will error on value imports that are erased at runtime.
rule.rationale: Prevents accidental inclusion of types-only modules in runtime bundles and makes the module graph explicit.

example.title: verbatimModuleSyntax import patterns
example.code: import type { User } from './types'; import { createUser } from './factory'; // value import — fine

section.name: react-19-next-js-15-python-3-13-2026-patterns
section.p: Server components/actions policy, Next.js 15 caching changes, Python 3.13 features, and ruff configuration.

rule.statement: Adopt server-components-by-default per route; add 'use client' only where interactivity is required — never sprinkle it at the module level without a documented per-route policy.
rule.rationale: Client boundaries prevent tree-shaking of server-only code; undisciplined use bloats the client bundle with dependencies that should never reach the browser.

rule.statement: Use Server Actions ('use server' in an async function) for form submissions and mutations; they run on the server, validate data there, and can access the database directly without an API route.
rule.rationale: Eliminates a client-to-API round-trip for simple mutations, removes the need to expose internal data shapes via an API, and colocates mutation logic with the form.

rule.statement: In Next.js 15, fetch() is no longer cached by default; explicitly opt in with cache: 'force-cache' or revalidate for data that should be cached.
rule.rationale: Next.js 15 reversed the default from cache-everything to no-cache. Code written for Next.js 14 that relies on implicit caching will fetch on every request in 15.

rule.statement: Pin Python at >=3.13,<3.14 in pyproject.toml until the next minor LTS stabilises; configure ruff with target-version = "py313" and select E, F, I, B, UP, SIM, T20, ASYNC.
rule.rationale: >=3.13 without an upper bound accepts future majors and breaks compatibility. ruff replaces black + isort + flake8 in a single binary; T20 catches print() statements left in production code.

example.title: Python 3.13 pyproject.toml configuration
example.code:
[project] requires-python = ">=3.13,<3.14"
[tool.ruff] target-version = "py313" line-length = 100
[tool.ruff.lint] select = ["E","F","I","B","UP","SIM","N","TCH","T20","ASYNC"]
[tool.mypy] python_version = "3.13" strict = true plugins = ["pydantic.mypy"]

rule.statement: Use built-in generic types (list[str], dict[str, int], tuple[int, ...]) instead of typing.List, typing.Dict; use X | None instead of Optional[X].
rule.rationale: PEP 585 and PEP 604 make the old typing module aliases deprecated. Mixing old and new in the same file causes inconsistent style that UP ruff rules will flag.

section.name: ai-assisted-development-tool-selection-and-guardrails
section.p: Criteria for choosing AI coding tools, safe prompt patterns, and guardrails for AI-generated test suites.

rule.statement: Choose the AI coding tool based on primary workflow: Claude Code for terminal-first, multi-file refactors and agent tasks; Cursor for IDE-native pair programming with codebase context; GitHub Copilot for line-completion embedded in any editor.
rule.rationale: Tool capabilities overlap but their UX models differ; mixing all three without a clear primary creates context fragmentation and inconsistent code style.

rule.statement: Require diff review on every AI-generated change; reject any AI-produced diff over 200 lines unreviewed as a single unit.
rule.rationale: AI models have consistent blind spots in the same training domains. Large unreviewed diffs obscure subtle logic errors under volume. 200 lines is approximately one focused review session.

antipattern.description: Accepting AI-generated code in bulk without review because "it looks right".
antipattern.reason: AI tools produce plausible-looking code that may silently introduce security issues, wrong algorithms, or subtle type errors that only surface at runtime.

rule.statement: Run the original test suite to establish a baseline before generating tests with AI; reject AI-generated tests that do not fail when reverting the change they test.
rule.rationale: AI-generated tests for AI-generated code share the same blind spots. A test that does not fail on revert provides no actual protection and creates false confidence in coverage.

rule.statement: Use a different model or prompt context to review AI-generated tests than was used to generate the code under test.
rule.rationale: The same model asked to generate code and tests will produce tests that mirror the implementation's assumptions, not independently verify correctness.

rule.statement: Run automated codemods (React 19 migration, TS 5 upgrades) per-package with a verification step after each package, never repo-wide in one shot.
rule.rationale: Codemods accumulate silent failures across packages; a per-package run catches and fixes each failure before it compounds into a large-scale rollback.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Two-phase load: (1) read the hub README.md to pick the relevant sub-doc(s); (2) read the sub-doc(s) and apply. For audits, pre-load all three; for targeted upgrades (e.g., "modernise TypeScript config"), load only modern-tooling-2026.md. Always cross-reference against the actual installed versions (package.json, pyproject.toml) before applying recommendations — the doc presumes current versions.

Recommended subagents: faion-improver — natural fit: it audits a system, finds gaps, proposes upgrades; this hub is its rubric. faion-sdd-executor-agent — executes the upgrade tasks once the gap list is produced. review skill — reviews the resulting modernisation PR.

Prompt pattern: Step 1 — load: best-practices-2026/README.md, best-practices-2026/code-quality-trends.md, best-practices-2026/modern-tooling-2026.md (TypeScript section only). Step 2 — audit ./apps/web (Next.js 14, TS 5.2, eslint-config-next): list deviations from "TypeScript 5 Strict Configuration", list missing React 19 / Next.js 15 features, propose phased upgrade plan as SDD tasks. Output: markdown report. Do not edit files yet.

section.name: limitations-and-gotchas
section.p: This is a navigation hub, not content. Agent that reads only README.md gets a TOC and zero substance — must follow links to the sub-docs to be useful.
"2026" snapshot ages quickly: TypeScript 5.x, React 19, Next.js 15, Python 3.13 are 2025-Q4 reality. New majors (React 20 / Python 3.14 / TS 6) will break section claims; cross-check release notes.
AI-coding section conflates capabilities of Copilot, Cursor, Claude Code — recommendations may not reflect current pricing/UX.
Sub-document sizes are estimated in tokens, not lines; agents using line-budget prompts mis-allocate context.
"Quick reference" code-quality section is brief and presents thresholds without source — agents will quote them as if authoritative.
Decomposed-doc pattern means the agent must do two reads to answer one question, increasing tool-call count.

Agent-specific gotchas: Hub-only read: the agent confidently reports recommendations from the TOC alone. Always require both the hub and the relevant sub-doc. Stale "2026" claims: agent quotes "TS 5.x, React 19" as if frozen. After a Node/React major release, agent should consult release notes, not just the doc. Tool-recommendation drift: pricing and feature set of Copilot/Cursor/Claude Code change quickly. Rephrase agent output as "as of doc revision date" rather than absolute. AI test generation: agents asked to write tests for AI-generated code re-use the same model's blind spots; run an independent reviewer (different prompt or different model) on the test suite. Codemod application: agent runs codemods (e.g., React 19) on the entire repo unattended; failures pile up silently. Run per-package with a verification step. Python typing: agent mixes typing.List (legacy) with list[T] (PEP 585) in the same file; force from __future__ import annotations or commit fully to PEP 585. Human-in-loop checkpoint: any change to tsconfig global strictness flags, eslint root config, or pyproject.toml lint set; these have repo-wide blast radius. "Recent enough" trap: agent that hasn't been told the current date applies 2024 best practices to a 2026 codebase. Always anchor with Today's date is YYYY-MM-DD in the prompt.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: "Currentness" is a maintenance practice, not a snapshot. Schedule a quarterly review against this hub; treat it as a rubric, not a constitution.
Adopt strict TS in new code first; convert legacy modules under // @ts-strict-check boundary or per-file pragmas; do not flip the global flag without budget.
React 19 server components: don't sprinkle 'use server' randomly; document a per-route policy (server-by-default, client-on-demand).
Pin Python at minor (>=3.13,<3.14) until next LTS-equivalent stabilises; agents using >=3.13 accept future majors and break compat.
AI-assisted dev: require diff-review on every AI-generated change; reject anything > 200 lines unreviewed. The doc is permissive; the team must be stricter.
For test generation by AI, run the original suite first to establish baseline; reject AI-added tests that don't fail when reverting the change (mutation-style sanity check).
Track DORA metrics + a small "AI-effectiveness" panel (PR cycle time pre/post AI tooling) — the Best Practices hub should pay back, not just feel good.

section.name: tools-and-services
section.p: CLI tools: npm-check-updates (ncu) (Detect outdated deps in JS/TS, npm i -g npm-check-updates), pip-audit / pip list --outdated (Python dep currency, pip install pip-audit), cargo outdated (Rust dep currency, cargo install cargo-outdated), bun outdated (Bun-native, bundled with bun), tsc --noEmit + tsconfig-strict-defaults (Validate TS strictness, bundled), ruff (Python) / eslint (JS) (2026 default linters, pip install ruff / npm i -D eslint), Codemod runners jscodeshift, lib-cra-codemod (Automated migrations e.g. React 19, npx @react-codemod), bunx / pnpm dlx (One-shot tool runs, bundled).

Services: Renovate / Dependabot (SaaS / OSS, Yes, Keeps the 2026 baseline current automatically), GitHub Copilot (SaaS, Yes, The "AI Coding Tool Selection" matrix slot), Cursor (SaaS, Yes — Composer + agents, Currently strongest IDE-native agent UX), Claude Code (SaaS, Yes — first-party, Subagents, hooks, skills this repo), SonarCloud (SaaS, Yes — REST, Track code-quality trends over time), Snyk / GitHub Security (SaaS, Yes — REST, Aligns with the Security Practices section), Sentry (SaaS / self-host, Yes, Performance + error budgets baseline), Vercel / Cloudflare Workers (SaaS, Yes, Targets for Next.js 15 / server actions).

template.purpose: TS 5 strict baseline with all 2026 recommended flags.
Python 3.13 + ruff + mypy strict baseline for pyproject.toml.
